project.title: The changes in HNSCC cell lines (e.g., OECM1) when treated with Cetuximab (500 ug/ml) at different passages. We used LC-MS/MS to identify the main target pathway. These findings could significantly enhance our evidence.
project.projectDescription: Recent clinical trials revealed that prior use of Cetuximab may interfere with ICB efficacy. In this study, STAT1 protein levels changed after continued Cetuximab treatment (500 ug/ml). We collected serial passages of cetuximab resistance HNSCC cell lines to perform LC-MS/MS. We identified the IFN pathway as the key target to cause this effect.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: In-sol digestion. Protein solutions were reconstituted in 50 mM ammonium bicarbonate (ABC, Sigma), followed by reduction of disulfide bonds using 10 mM dithiothreitol (DTT, Merck) at 56°C for 45 min. Continue to cysteine alkylation with 55mM of iodoacetamide (IAM, Sigma) at 25°C for 30 min. Digestion was performed by adding sequencing-grade modified porcine trypsin (Promega) at a protein-to-trypsin ratio of 20:1. The samples were then placed in a 37°C incubator for overnight digestion. Subsequently, the peptides were desalted, dried using a vacuum centrifuge, and stored at -80°C until further use.  LC-MS/MS analysis. The digested peptides were dissolved in HPLC buffer A (0.1% formic acid) and subjected to a reverse-phase column (Zorbax 300SB-C18, 0.3 × 5 mm; Agilent Technologies). Chromatographic separation of the peptides was achieved using a column (Waters BEH 1.7 μm, 100 μm I.D. × 10 cm with a 15 μm tip) by gradient elution with HPLC buffer B (99.9% acetonitrile/0.1% formic acid) over 70 minutes with a flow rate of 0.3 μl/min. Coupling the LC apparatus was a 2D linear ion trap mass spectrometer (Orbitrap Elite ETD; Thermo Fisher) operated using Xcalibur 2.2 software (Thermo Fisher). Full-scan spectra were acquired covering a mass range of 400 to 2,000 Da at a fixed resolution of 120,000 at m/z 400. The ion signal of protonated dodecamethylcyclohexasiloxane at m/z 536.165365 was used as a lock mass for internal calibration. Precursor ions were isolated using a dynamic exclusion window (DEW) of 40 seconds with a relative mass window of 15 ppm. The 20 most abundant precursor ions are fragmented by data-dependent MS/MS scans followed by the previous MS scan. The electrospray voltage was set to 2.0 kV, and the temperature of the capillary was set to 200°C. The automatic gain control was set as 1,000 ms (full scan) and 200 ms (MS/MS), or 3 × 106 ions (full scan) and 3,000 ions (MS/MS) for maximum accumulated time and ions respectively.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Protein identification and label-free quantification. The Data analysis was performed using Proteome Discoverer software (version 2.3, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Mascot search engine (Matrix Science, London, UK; version 2.5) was employed to search each MS/MS spectrum against the SwissProt database. For peptide identification, a mass tolerance of 10 ppm was allowed for intact peptide masses, while 0.5 Da was permitted for CID fragment ions and up to 2 missed cleavages sites resulting from trypsin digestion was allowed. Oxidized methionine and acetyl (protein N-terminal) were considered as variable modifications; carbamidomethyl (cysteine) were considered as static modification. Peptide-spectrum matches (PSMs) were subsequently filtered based on high confidence and Mascot search engine rank 1 peptide, ensuring an overall false discovery rate below 0.01. Proteins identified with at least two peptide hit were selected. The relative quantification of protein was based on the sum of peptide peak area, detected using Minora algorithm. The differential proteins were then determined using threshold: mean of log2 transformed ratio ± 1 × standard deviation of log2 transformed ratio, assuming normalized ratio distributed normally.
